# AIchitect

> AI tools are all over the place. AIchitect maps the full ecosystem so developers can pick the right stack without the noise.

AIchitect is a free, open-source, interactive tool landscape for AI developers. It catalogs 111 AI tools across 11 categories and maps their integrations, so teams can understand the ecosystem and make informed stack decisions.

GitHub: https://github.com/letsgogeeky/aichitect

## What AIchitect covers

- **111 AI tools** spanning code editors, agent frameworks, LLM providers, observability, vector databases, MCP servers, and more
- **11 tool categories**: Coding Assistants, Autonomous Agents, Agent Frameworks, Pipelines & RAG, LLM Infrastructure, Design & UI, DevOps & CI/CD, Documentation, Product & PM, MCP Servers, Prompt & Eval
- **3 relationship types** between tools: integrates-with, often used together, competes-with
- **243 mapped relationships** showing which tools integrate, pair well, or compete
- **8 curated stacks** covering common AI engineering patterns (Indie Hacker, Enterprise RAG, OSS Self-Hosted, Agentic DevOps, and more)
- **Stack Builder**: compose a custom stack slot-by-slot and see live integration graphs with shareable URLs
- **Tool Comparison**: compare any two tools side by side — pricing, integrations, shared ecosystem connections, and direct relationships

## Pages

- / — Landing page with links to all three views
- /explore — Full interactive graph of all 111 tools with category and relationship filters; supports 2D grid, 2D layers, and 3D rotation views; supports compare mode via URL
- /stacks — 8 battle-tested, pre-built AI stacks visualized as swimlane integration graphs
- /builder — Build a custom stack by picking one tool per slot; renders live connection graph; share via URL
- /compare/[tool-a]/[tool-b] — Dedicated comparison page for any two tools; server-rendered with full pricing, relationship, and ecosystem data

## URL patterns

- /explore?compare=cursor,github-copilot — compare two tools in the interactive graph
- /compare/cursor/github-copilot — dedicated SEO page comparing two specific tools
- /builder?s=cursor,langgraph,openai-api — pre-loaded stack in the builder
- /explore?s=cursor — explore page with a specific tool highlighted

## Tool comparison: key pairs

- Cursor vs GitHub Copilot: /compare/cursor/github-copilot
- LangChain vs LlamaIndex: /compare/langchain/llamaindex
- OpenAI API vs Anthropic API: /compare/openai-api/anthropic-api
- CrewAI vs AutoGen: /compare/crewai/autogen
- Devin vs OpenHands: /compare/devin/openhands
- Pinecone vs Weaviate: /compare/pinecone/weaviate (if relationship exists)
- LiteLLM vs OpenRouter: /compare/litellm/openrouter

## Tool categories and example tools

- Coding Assistants: Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Zed, VS Code
- Autonomous Agents: Devin, OpenHands, Claude Code, Aider, Plandex, Goose
- Agent Frameworks: LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, LlamaIndex, Semantic Kernel
- Pipelines & RAG: LangChain, Dify, n8n, Flowise
- LLM Infrastructure: OpenAI API, Anthropic API, Together AI, Fireworks AI, Ollama, llama.cpp, LiteLLM, OpenRouter
- Observability & Eval: LangSmith, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, Helicone, RAGAS
- MCP Servers: MCP SDK Python, MCP SDK TypeScript, PortKey
- Design & UI: v0, Vercel v0, Figma AI
- DevOps & CI/CD: Modal, Render, Railway, Fly.io
- Documentation: Mintlify, Readme.io

## Data shape (for programmatic use)

Each tool has: id, name, category, tagline, description, type (oss|commercial), pricing (free_tier, plans[]), github_stars, urls (website, github)

Each relationship has: source (tool id), target (tool id), type (integrates-with|commonly-paired|competes-with)

## Brand

AIchitect's core thesis: the AI tooling space is noisy and overwhelming. Developers waste time evaluating tools that don't work well together. AIchitect solves this with a structured, visual map of the ecosystem — so you can pick your stack based on real integration data, not hype.

Tagline: "Cut the noise. Pick your AI stack."

## Open Source

AIchitect is MIT-licensed and fully open source. Contributions, issues, and new tool suggestions are welcome via GitHub.
